L02 is the hands-on foundation of this course. You will learn to navigate the Linux filesystem, manipulate files, understand permissions, and chain commands together with pipes. Every concept here is used daily by professional developers and system administrators. Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, S. Teerakanok, MUIC

Feel free to reach out with questions after class or via email. Office hours by appointment.

These slides are designed for self-study. Speaker notes provide deeper explanation. When in doubt, check the manual pages (man command) or official documentation.

This is a dense lecture covering essential Linux skills. We move from filesystem concepts through file operations, then to the powerful pipe/redirection system, and finish with users and permissions. Follow along in your terminal. Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, S. Teerakanok, MUIC

The single most important design principle in UNIX/Linux. Understanding this philosophy is the key to understanding how Linux works. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

This is the foundational UNIX philosophy: the OS exposes everything through the file abstraction. Regular files, directories, hardware devices, network sockets, and even running processes are all accessed through the same file interface. This means the same tools work everywhere. cat reads files — but it also reads device files and virtual filesystems. echo writes text — and it can write to device files. The redirection operator > works on regular files and special files alike. Example: cat /proc/cpuinfo reads CPU information using the same cat command you use for text files. The /proc filesystem is a "virtual filesystem" — it does not exist on disk. The kernel generates the content on the fly when you read it. This uniformity is what makes Linux so powerful and composable. You do not need a special "device info" tool — the standard file tools already work. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

Linux has seven file types, each indicated by the first character of the ls -l output. The most common are regular files (-), directories (d), and symbolic links (l). Block devices (b): Represent storage devices like hard drives and SSDs. Data is read/written in fixed-size blocks. Character devices (c): Represent devices that handle data as a stream of characters — terminals, serial ports. Named pipes (p) and sockets (s): Used for inter-process communication (IPC). Pipes allow one process to send data to another. Sockets allow network communication. The file command inspects a file's actual contents (magic bytes) to determine its type, regardless of the file extension: file document.pdf → "PDF document, version 1.7". Linux does not rely on file extensions to determine file type — a file named report.txt could contain a JPEG image. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

Linux has one unified tree starting at / (root) — no drive letters like Windows. External drives and USB sticks are "mounted" as subdirectories within this tree. Key directories to memorize: /home/username (or ~): Your personal files. Each user has their own directory here. /etc: System-wide configuration. SSH config, user accounts (/etc/passwd), hostname, network settings — all live here. /var/log: System and application logs. This is the first place to check when debugging problems. /dev/null: The "black hole" — any data written to it is discarded. Useful for suppressing unwanted output: command > /dev/null /tmp: Temporary files, cleared on reboot. Programs use this for scratch space. /proc: A virtual filesystem — not stored on disk. The kernel generates content on the fly. /proc/cpuinfo shows CPU info, /proc/meminfo shows memory info. Source: Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) 3.0; OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 11

Now that you understand the directory tree, let us learn how to move around it. Source: GNU Coreutils manual; OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10-11

There are two ways to specify a file location: Absolute path: Starts with / (the root). It specifies the complete path from the root of the filesystem. Example: /home/student/hw1.c always refers to the same file, no matter where you currently are. Relative path: Starts from your current working directory. Uses special symbols: - . (dot) = current directory - .. (double dot) = parent directory (one level up) - ~ (tilde) = home directory (/home/username) - - (hyphen with cd) = previous directory Example: If you are in /home/student, then hw1.c refers to /home/student/hw1.c. And ../projects/hw1 means "go up one level to /home, then into projects/hw1." Rule of thumb: Use absolute paths in scripts (they always work regardless of where the script runs). Use relative paths interactively (they are shorter to type). Source: GNU Coreutils manual

Source: Generated by gen-screenshots.py (Pillow) — bash terminal session: pwd, ls -la, cd

pwd (Print Working Directory): Shows your current location in the filesystem. Use it whenever you are unsure where you are. cd (Change Directory): Moves you to a different directory. cd without arguments takes you to your home directory (~). cd - takes you to the previous directory you were in. ls (List): Shows files and directories. Without arguments, shows the current directory. The most commonly used flags: - -l: Long format — shows permissions, owner, group, size, date, and name - -a: Shows ALL files including hidden ones (files starting with a dot, like .bashrc) - -la: Combines both — the most useful form Hidden files in Linux start with a dot (.). Files like .bashrc, .profile, .ssh/ are hidden by default. They are used for configuration and are hidden to reduce clutter. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

The ls -l output contains a lot of information packed into one line. Let us decode each field: 1. File type: The first character. d = directory, - = regular file, l = symbolic link. 2. Permissions: Nine characters in three groups of three (rwx). The first group is for the owner, the second for the group, and the third for everyone else. r = read, w = write, x = execute. A dash means that permission is not granted. 3. Hard link count: Number of hard links pointing to this file. For directories, this includes . and .. entries. 4. Owner: The user who owns the file. 5. Group: The group that owns the file. 6. Size: File size in bytes. For directories, this is the size of the directory metadata, not the total size of contained files. 7. Date: Last modification date and time. 8. Name: The file or directory name. You will use ls -l constantly throughout this course. Memorize what each field means. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

The core file manipulation commands. Practice these until they become muscle memory. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

touch: If the file does not exist, it creates an empty file. If it already exists, it updates the modification timestamp without changing the content. The dual purpose makes it useful for both file creation and timestamp manipulation. mkdir: Creates a new directory. Without -p, it fails if the parent directory does not exist. With -p, it creates the entire directory chain: mkdir -p a/b/c creates a/, a/b/, and a/b/c/ all at once. The -p flag stands for "parents" — create parent directories as needed. You will use mkdir -p frequently in scripts to ensure the target directory exists. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

rm (remove): Permanently deletes files. Unlike a graphical file manager, there is no trash can or recycle bin. Once you rm a file, it is gone. Key flags: - -i: Interactive mode — asks for confirmation before each deletion. Useful safety net. - -r: Recursive — required to delete directories and their contents. - -f: Force — suppresses all confirmation prompts and ignores non-existent files. - -rf: The most dangerous combination. Recursively deletes everything without asking. rmdir: Only removes empty directories. If the directory contains any files, it fails. This is safer than rm -r for cleaning up. CRITICAL WARNING: rm -rf / would attempt to delete your entire filesystem. Always double-check the path before pressing Enter. A common accident is typing rm -rf . / (with a space) instead of rm -rf ./ — the former deletes the current directory AND the root filesystem. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

cp (copy): Creates a duplicate of a file. For directories, you must use -r (recursive) to copy the directory and all its contents. mv (move): Serves double duty — it both renames (when the destination is in the same directory) and moves (when the destination is a different directory). Under the hood, a rename is actually a move within the same filesystem. Examples: - mv oldname.txt newname.txt — renames the file (same directory) - mv file.txt /home/student/ — moves the file to a different location - mv file.txt /home/student/newname.txt — moves AND renames in one step Unlike cp, mv does not need a -r flag for directories — it simply updates the directory entry, which is almost instantaneous regardless of directory size. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

cat (concatenate): Prints the entire file to the terminal. Good for short files, but impractical for large ones (output scrolls by too fast). less: A pager — lets you scroll up and down through a file. Press q to quit, / to search, n for next match. Much better than cat for large files. head: Shows the first N lines. Default is 10 lines. Use head -n 20 data.csv to preview the first 20 lines of a CSV file. tail: Shows the last N lines. Especially useful for log files, where the most recent entries are at the bottom. tail -f (follow): Keeps the file open and displays new lines as they are written in real-time. Invaluable for monitoring: tail -f /var/log/auth.log lets you watch authentication events as they happen. Press Ctrl+C to stop. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

Wildcards let you operate on multiple files at once without typing each name. The shell performs the expansion before the command sees the arguments. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 3.5.8

The shell expands wildcard characters before passing arguments to commands. This is called "globbing." * (asterisk): Matches zero or more characters. ls *.c matches main.c, utils.c, test.c — any file ending in .c. ? (question mark): Matches exactly one character. ls file?.txt matches file1.txt and fileA.txt, but NOT file10.txt (because 10 is two characters). [...] (bracket expression): Matches one character from the set. ls [0-9]* matches files starting with a digit. [abc] matches a, b, or c. [a-z] matches any lowercase letter. [!...] (negated bracket): Matches one character NOT in the set. ls [!A-Z]* matches files that do NOT start with an uppercase letter. IMPORTANT: The shell performs globbing, not the command. When you type ls *.c, the shell expands *.c to main.c utils.c test.c and then runs ls main.c utils.c test.c. The ls program never sees the asterisk. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 3.5.8 (Filename Expansion)

The man command is the single most important reference tool in Linux. Learn to use it and you can teach yourself any command. Source: man-pages project, man(1)

The man command opens the manual page for any command, system call, or library function. Manual pages are organized into numbered sections. Section 1 contains user commands (what you type in the shell). Section 2 contains system calls (kernel interface). Section 3 contains C library functions. Why sections matter: Some names exist in multiple sections. For example, printf exists as a shell command (section 1) and a C library function (section 3). man printf shows section 1 by default. man 3 printf shows the C function. Navigation inside man: - Arrow keys or j/k: scroll up/down - /pattern: search forward for "pattern" - n: jump to next search match - q: quit and return to shell Pro tip: If you do not know the exact command name, use man -k keyword (equivalent to apropos keyword) to search all manual pages for a keyword. For example: man -k "copy files" might lead you to the cp manual. Source: man-pages project, man(1)

This is the most important section of L02. Redirection and pipes embody the UNIX philosophy: small, focused programs chained together to solve complex tasks. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 3.4; GNU Bash manual

Every process in Linux has three default I/O streams, each identified by a number called a file descriptor (fd): stdin (fd 0): Standard input. By default, this is the keyboard — what you type goes into the program. stdout (fd 1): Standard output. By default, this is the terminal — normal output from the program appears on your screen. stderr (fd 2): Standard error. Also defaults to the terminal, but is a separate stream from stdout. This separation lets you redirect errors to a different destination than normal output. These three streams are the foundation of the UNIX I/O model. Redirection and pipes work by changing where these streams point — instead of the keyboard and terminal, they can point to files or other programs. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 3.4

Output redirection changes where stdout (or stderr) goes. > (overwrite): Creates the file if it does not exist. If it already exists, it erases all existing content and writes the new output. This is the most common mistake — students use > when they meant >> and lose their existing data. >> (append): Creates the file if it does not exist. If it already exists, it adds the new output to the end without erasing anything. 2> (stderr redirection): Redirects only error messages. The 2 refers to file descriptor 2 (stderr). Normal output still goes to the terminal. &> (both): Shorthand for redirecting both stdout and stderr to the same file. Equivalent to > file 2>&1 (redirect stdout to file, then redirect stderr to the same place as stdout). Example: command > output.txt 2> errors.txt sends normal output to output.txt and errors to errors.txt — separating them for easy debugging. Source: GNU Bash manual

Input redirection (<): Takes the contents of a file and feeds it as standard input to a command. sort < unsorted.txt reads unsorted.txt as input to sort, instead of reading from the keyboard. Pipes (|): The pipe operator connects the stdout of one command to the stdin of the next. This is the most powerful concept in UNIX — it lets you chain simple programs together to solve complex problems. ls | more: The output of ls becomes the input of more (a pager). ls -la | grep config: The output of ls -la is searched by grep for lines containing "config." cat file | wc: The contents of file are counted by wc (word count — shows lines, words, and characters). Each program in a pipeline runs as a separate process. They run simultaneously — the second program starts reading as soon as the first produces output. This is efficient: you do not need to wait for the first command to finish before the second starts. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 3.4; GNU Bash manual

This example shows the power of pipelines. Three simple commands chained together solve a practical problem: finding the largest log files. Breaking it down: 1. du -sh /var/log/* — calculates disk usage for each item in /var/log. -s = summary (total per item, not per file inside). -h = human-readable sizes (10M instead of 10485760). 2. sort -rh — sorts the output. -r = reverse (largest first). -h = human-readable numeric sort (understands K, M, G suffixes). 3. head -5 — shows only the first 5 lines (the 5 largest items). This embodies the UNIX philosophy: small, focused programs (du, sort, head) that each do one thing well, composed via pipes to solve complex tasks. No single command does all of this — but the combination is powerful. Real-world use: System administrators use pipelines like this daily to analyze logs, find large files, filter process lists, and extract data. Source: GNU Bash manual

RC (Run Commands) files configure your shell automatically each time a new session starts. Understanding them is key to a productive terminal workflow. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 6.2

Shell startup files run automatically each time a new shell starts. They contain customizations: Aliases: Shortcuts for common commands. alias ll='ls -la' lets you type ll instead of ls -la. alias gs='git status' saves even more keystrokes. Environment variables: export EDITOR=vim sets your default text editor. export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH" adds ~/bin to the beginning of your command search path. .bashrc vs .bash_profile: .bashrc runs for every new interactive non-login shell (e.g., opening a new terminal tab). .bash_profile runs only for login shells (e.g., SSH sessions, first terminal after login). In practice, many users source .bashrc from .bash_profile so their settings are consistent. After editing a startup file, you need to either open a new terminal or run source ~/.bashrc to apply the changes in your current session. source reads and executes the file in the current shell. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 6.2 (Bash Startup Files)

Linux is a multi-user system. The permission model controls access to every file and directory. Understanding this is essential for security and collaboration. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

Linux is a multi-user system. Every user has: - A username and UID (numeric user ID) — the kernel uses the UID internally - A primary group and optionally additional groups - A home directory (/home/username) Groups enable shared access. If Alice and Bob are both in the "project" group, they can share files using group permissions. A user can belong to multiple groups. whoami: Prints your current username. Simple but useful when you SSH into different servers and need to confirm which account you are using. id: Shows your UID, primary GID (Group ID), and all groups you belong to. The output looks like: uid=1000(student) gid=1000(student) groups=1000(student),27(sudo) groups: Lists just the group names you belong to. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

The superuser (root) is the most privileged user on a Linux system, always with UID 0. Root can override all permission settings — read any file, kill any process, modify any system configuration. su (switch user): Switches to the root user for an entire session. Requires the root password. This is risky because you might accidentally run destructive commands as root. sudo (superuser do): Runs a single command as root, then returns to your normal user. Requires YOUR password (not root's), and only works if your user is in the sudoers file. This is much safer than su because: 1. Temporary: privilege lasts for one command only 2. Audited: every sudo command is logged 3. Granular: sudoers can restrict which commands a user may run Best practice: Always use sudo instead of su. Only use root privileges when absolutely necessary (installing packages, modifying system config, reading protected files). Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

Each file has three sets of permissions, each applying to a different audience: Owner (u for user): The file's creator or owner. Typically the person who created the file. Group (g): Members of the file's group. All users in this group share these permissions. Other (o): Everyone else — any user who is neither the owner nor in the group. Each set has three permission bits: Read (r): For files, allows viewing the contents. For directories, allows listing the contents with ls. Write (w): For files, allows modifying the contents. For directories, allows creating and deleting files inside the directory. Execute (x): For files, allows running the file as a program. For directories, allows entering the directory with cd. Note: you need BOTH read AND execute permission to fully use a directory — read to list its contents, execute to enter it. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

Permission strings are read in groups of three characters after the file type indicator. The first character is the file type (d = directory, - = regular file, l = symlink). Then three groups of three characters each: owner, group, other. Each position is either the permission letter (r, w, x) or a dash (-) meaning that permission is denied. Octal notation assigns a number to each permission: r=4, w=2, x=1. Add them up for each group: - rwx = 4+2+1 = 7 (full access) - r-x = 4+0+1 = 5 (read and execute) - r-- = 4+0+0 = 4 (read only) - --- = 0+0+0 = 0 (no access) Common octal values: - 755: rwxr-xr-x — owner full, others read+execute (typical for directories and scripts) - 644: rw-r--r-- — owner read+write, others read only (typical for regular files) - 700: rwx------ — owner full, no access for anyone else (private files) - 777: rwxrwxrwx — full access for everyone (AVOID in production!) Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10

chmod (change mode) modifies file permissions. It supports two notations: Octal mode: Specify the complete permission set as a three-digit number. chmod 755 script.sh sets owner=rwx, group=r-x, other=r-x. chmod 644 document.txt sets owner=rw-, group=r--, other=r--. Symbolic mode: Modify specific permissions. Format is [who][operator][permission]. Who: u = owner (user), g = group, o = other, a = all (everyone). Operator: + = add permission, - = remove permission, = = set exactly. Permission: r = read, w = write, x = execute. Examples: - chmod u+x script.sh — add execute permission for the owner - chmod g-w file.txt — remove write permission for the group - chmod o-rwx private.txt — remove all permissions for others - chmod a+r readme.txt — add read permission for everyone Common question: "How do I make a script executable?" Answer: chmod u+x script.sh (or chmod +x script.sh to add execute for everyone). Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10; GNU Coreutils manual

chown (change owner) modifies who owns a file. Syntax: chown [owner][:group] file sudo chown alice file.txt — changes the file owner to alice. Only root can change ownership, so sudo is required. sudo chown alice:developers file.txt — changes both the owner (to alice) and the group (to developers) in one command. sudo chown :developers file.txt — changes only the group. The colon before the group name with no user means "keep the current owner." sudo chown -R alice:devs project/ — recursive mode. Applies the ownership change to the directory and everything inside it (all subdirectories and files). Only root (or sudo) can change file ownership. Regular users can only change group ownership to groups they belong to, using chgrp instead. Source: OSC, Silberschatz et al., 9th Ed., Ch 10; GNU Coreutils manual

Understanding PATH explains why some commands work and others give "command not found" — and why ./script.sh requires the ./ prefix. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 3.7.2

PATH is an environment variable containing a colon-separated list of directories. When you type a command, the shell searches these directories from left to right until it finds a matching executable. which command: Shows the full path of the executable the shell would run. Useful for debugging "which version am I using?" situations. Why ./script.sh? The current directory (.) is intentionally NOT in PATH by default. This is a security measure. If . were in PATH, an attacker could place a malicious ls program in your current directory, and it would run instead of /bin/ls. To run a program not in PATH: - Use its full path: /home/student/myscript.sh - Use relative path: ./myscript.sh To add a directory to PATH permanently, add this line to ~/.bashrc: export PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH" Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 3.7.2 (Command Search and Execution)

A brief introduction to process management commands. Full coverage is in L03. This section bridges L01's concept of processes with practical commands. Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, Slides 35-37

ps (process status): Shows a snapshot of running processes. Without options, only shows processes in your current terminal session. ps aux shows ALL processes on the system. The flags a, u, x mean: a = all users, u = user-oriented format (shows owner, CPU%, MEM%), x = include processes without a controlling terminal (background daemons). pstree: Visualizes the parent-child relationships between processes as a tree. Every process has a parent (except PID 1, the init/systemd process). When you type a command, your shell creates a child process to run it. top: An interactive, real-time process monitor. Shows CPU usage, memory usage, and running processes updated every few seconds. Press q to quit, k to kill a process, M to sort by memory, P to sort by CPU. htop is an enhanced version with colors and mouse support (may need to install separately). Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, Slides 35-37

kill PID: Sends SIGTERM (signal 15) to the process. This is a polite request to terminate — the process can catch it and clean up (close files, release resources) before exiting. kill -9 PID: Sends SIGKILL (signal 9). This is a forced kill that cannot be caught or ignored. The process is terminated immediately without any cleanup. Only use this as a last resort. Ctrl+C: Sends SIGINT (interrupt) to the foreground process. Most programs treat this as "cancel and exit." Ctrl+Z: Sends SIGTSTP (terminal stop) to the foreground process. This pauses the process without killing it. You can resume it later with fg (foreground) or bg (background). jobs: Lists all jobs (suspended or background) in the current shell. Key insight: Ctrl+C kills the process. Ctrl+Z pauses it. You can resume a paused job with fg or bg. This is how you can manage multiple tasks in a single terminal. Full coverage of process management is in L03. Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, Slides 35-37

These tools and shortcuts save enormous time. Learn them early and use them daily. Source: GNU Coreutils manual; GNU Bash manual, Section 8

wget: Downloads a file from a URL and saves it to disk. Simple and straightforward. curl: More flexible HTTP client. curl -O downloads and saves with the original filename. curl can also send POST requests, add headers, and handle authentication. tar (tape archive): Packs multiple files/directories into a single archive file. Common flag combinations: - -czf = create + gzip compression + filename (create archive) - -xzf = extract + gzip + filename (extract archive) - -tzf = list + gzip + filename (view contents without extracting) - -v = verbose (show files as they are processed) The flags: c = create, x = extract, z = gzip compression, f = filename (must be last flag), t = list contents, v = verbose. zip/unzip: The .zip format common on Windows. zip creates, unzip extracts. Source: GNU Coreutils manual

Tab completion: The single biggest time-saver. Type the first few characters of a file or command name, press Tab, and the shell completes it for you. If multiple matches exist, press Tab twice to see all options. This prevents typos and saves enormous time. Ctrl+R (reverse search): Press Ctrl+R, then start typing part of a previous command. Bash searches your command history and shows the most recent match. Press Ctrl+R again to find older matches. Press Enter to run it. This is the most useful shortcut for developers. Ctrl+C vs Ctrl+Z: Ctrl+C sends SIGINT (kills the program). Ctrl+Z sends SIGTSTP (pauses it). Use Ctrl+C when you want to stop something. Use Ctrl+Z when you want to pause it temporarily. !!: Expands to the last command. The most common use: apt update fails with "permission denied" → type sudo !! to rerun it with sudo. Ctrl+A / Ctrl+E: Jump to the beginning / end of the command line. Useful for editing long commands. Source: GNU Bash manual, Section 8 (Command Line Editing)

These are the essential concepts from L02: 1. The "everything is a file" philosophy means the same tools (cat, echo, >) work on regular files, device files, and virtual filesystems. This uniformity is the foundation of Linux's power. 2. Absolute paths (starting with /) work from anywhere. Relative paths depend on your current directory. Use absolute paths in scripts for reliability. 3. rm permanently deletes files. There is no trash can. Always double-check before pressing Enter, especially with rm -rf. 4. Pipes are the UNIX superpower. They let you chain simple programs together to solve complex problems. Each program in the pipeline runs as a separate process. 5. File permissions follow the rwx model across three audiences (owner, group, other). Learn to read and set permissions in both symbolic (chmod u+x) and octal (chmod 755) notation. 6. PATH is a colon-separated list of directories the shell searches for commands. This is why ./script.sh needs the ./ prefix — your current directory is not in PATH by default. 7. Tab completion and Ctrl+R (history search) dramatically improve terminal productivity. Start using them immediately. Source: [ICCS121] L02 - Linux Essentials.pdf, S. Teerakanok, MUIC

Thank you for attending. Please review the speaker notes for self-study. Next lecture (L03) covers Process Management and Shell Scripting — managing processes, writing bash scripts, conditionals, and loops.
